turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to understand how you can actually use boolean logic at the type level instead of just runtime flags. It feels like magic compared to using standard if statements or environment variables, and I keep hitting walls when I try to combine conditions.

It stops feeling like magic once you stop thinking about it as a feature and start seeing it as the compiler doing compile-time boolean evaluation. Instead of runtime flags, you're using phantom types where True and False are just distinct type variants that get combined through traits like And<A, B>. The beauty is that when your code matches these specific trait implementations, the compiler guarantees those conditions are met at compile time rather than runtime.

So I should be using phantom types to represent True and False instead of standard booleans, right? But how do I actually combine them together like And<A, B> without writing out all these boilerplate trait implementations myself?

You absolutely need to use phantom types for True and False rather than standard booleans because you want the logic baked into the type system itself. For combining conditions with traits like And<A, B>, you can leverage existing crates in the ecosystem that provide pre-built trait implementations so you don't have to write everything from scratch yourself.

I've been looking at this crate called boolum which seems to handle most of these cases automatically. But I'm still confused about how it actually generates the conditional compilation logic internally and if there are any performance implications compared to manual trait implementations.

Boolum handles the heavy lifting by generating the necessary trait implementations for you, which means your code stays clean while the compiler does the work of combining those phantom types together. There's a negligible performance difference because the logic is resolved at compile time anyway, so runtime overhead remains zero regardless of whether you use boolum or manually implement the traits yourself.

That makes sense, but I'm still stuck on how to actually write my own conditional compilation logic if I decide not to use any crates and want to understand the raw mechanics under the hood. Can you walk me through setting up a minimal example with just basic phantom types?

Sure, start by defining your True and False variants as separate type definitions that act like boolean values but live entirely in the type system. Then implement the And<A, B> trait where the implementation simply checks if both A and B are true using those phantom types you defined earlier.
